moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Grob Opening, a rare but provocative system where we immediately challenge Black's center with a flank pawn instead of occupying it ourselves. By pushing the g-pawn forward, we control the h5 and f5 squares while preparing to fianchetto our bishop to put pressure on the long diagonal—this is a hypermodern approach that prioritizes piece activity and spatial control over classical central occupation. The move is unorthodox and catches many opponents off guard, though it does weaken our kingside, so we need to be tactically alert. Historically, this opening has been championed by aggressive players looking for dynamic imbalance rather than theoretical soundness.

Black strikes back immediately with the Alessi Gambit, meeting our flank aggression with a central break that opens lines and grabs space in the center. By pushing f5, Black contests the e4 square (which we might want for our bishop after fianchettoing), creates tension in the position, and prepares to advance the e-pawn or defend the center with pieces; this is a principled response that doesn't allow us a slow, methodical buildup. Black accepts that the f5 pawn becomes somewhat weak and exposed, but gains dynamic counterplay and central influence—a typical gambit theme where material or pawn structure is sacrificed for initiative.
